items.q: How does a harmonic minor scale differ from a natural minor?
items.correct: The seventh degree is raised by a semitone (creating a leading note).
items.misconception: All notes are raised by a semitone.
items.why: Students think the whole scale changes. Only the 7TH is altered to create a leading note, which strengthens cadences in minor keys - producing the characteristic augmented second between 6th and 7th.

items.correct: Three sections: A, contrasting B, and a return to A (ABA).
items.why: Students assume 'three sections' means three different ones. Ternary has only TWO distinct ideas, but A returns - it's the return that makes it ternary, not the section count alone.

items.q: Which chords form a plagal cadence?
items.why: Students confuse plagal (IV-I) with perfect (V-I) because both end on I. The 'amen' at the end of hymns is the classic plagal cadence - subdominant to tonic, with a softer ecclesiastical feel.
It's Schoenberg. “Stravinsky” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which composer is most associated with minimalist works such as 'Music for 18 Musicians'?
items.why: It's Reich. “Glass” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year did John Cage compose his silent work '4'33"'?
items.why: The real figure is 1952. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
